title: Shifting Platform Development from Projects to Products
gist: A KubeCon case study from a game-streaming platform team on why yearly project-based platform planning breaks once a platform serves multiple internal tenants, and the shift to a product model behaving like an internal cloud provider with self-service APIs and clear tenancy boundaries.

themes.theme: Why project-thinking breaks at platform scale

insights.claim: Organizing platform development around yearly project planning with upfront scoping and fixed milestones works only while the platform serves a single service; once it serves multiple internal teams with divergent needs, the nature of the system fundamentally changes.
insights.speaker: Ben Linders
insights.so_what: A leading indicator that a platform org needs restructuring is multi-tenant adoption outgrowing single-service origins.
insights.quote: Over time, our platform evolved to support multiple internal teams with different needs. That fundamentally changed the nature of the system.
insights.locator: Bergman mentioned that their platform development cycle

insights.claim: Project-based platform delivery optimized for completion rather than long-term value: each improvement was a one-off delivery, capabilities competed in priority with the next delivery item, and feedback loops were internal sprint metrics with little validation from actual platform users.
insights.speaker: Ben Linders
insights.so_what: Sprint-velocity metrics without user-adoption signals are a warning sign a platform is delivering without improving coherence.
insights.quote: The system optimized for completion rather than for long-term value.

insights.claim: The concrete signals forcing the shift were backlogs growing despite continuous delivery, teams working around the platform instead of using it, and inability to answer whether what was built was actually being used.
insights.speaker: Ben Linders
insights.so_what: These three symptoms are a diagnostic checklist for platform teams to detect project-thinking failure before scaling.
insights.quote: Teams were working around the platform instead of using it
insights.locator: The shift was not driven by a single decision

themes.theme: Product model as internal cloud provider

insights.claim: A mature developer platform behaves like an internal cloud provider, exposing Kubernetes clusters, storage, and networking through self-service Kubernetes-native APIs and custom resources so developers provision infrastructure as code and avoid ticket-based coordination.
insights.speaker: Ben Linders
insights.so_what: The internal-cloud-provider mental model is the target-state architecture buyers of platform-engineering tooling should benchmark against.
insights.quote: At a high level, our developer platform behaves like an internal cloud provider.
insights.locator: InfoQ: How does your developer platform look?

insights.claim: The platform separates concerns across service, control, and management planes with composable building blocks and contracts defining stable interfaces between layers, rather than being a single unified platform.
insights.speaker: Ben Linders
insights.so_what: Layered plane separation with stable contracts is the reference architecture for decoupled multi-tenant platforms.
insights.locator: Internally, the platform is structured around a separation of concerns

insights.claim: The team evolved from Helm-based deployments, namespace-scoped resources, and a GitLab-driven workflow toward higher-level environment and project constructs and API-driven workflows aligned with cloud service provider paradigms after adopting public cloud services like AWS shifted team expectations toward self-service.
insights.speaker: Ben Linders
insights.so_what: Public-cloud self-service expectations pull internal platforms toward higher abstractions; vendors should target this migration path.
insights.locator: As their ecosystem expanded, especially with the introduction of public cloud services

insights.claim: The shift from project to product was catalyzed by systems- and product-thinking peers drawing on Prometheus and Grafana ecosystems and internal engagement with the books Project to Product and The Phoenix Project.
insights.speaker: Ben Linders
insights.so_what: The change was influence-driven, not tool-driven — cultural adoption of product thinking precedes platform re-architecture.
insights.locator: Inspired by a mix of external influence and internal reflection

insights.claim: Long-standing skepticism toward Scrum drove the product shift because Scrum's structure often failed to capture real outcomes, with product thinking offering better alignment between engineering work, user value, and team experience.
insights.speaker: Ben Linders
insights.so_what: A named critique that Scrum optimizes output over outcomes — relevant to how platform orgs measure success.
insights.quote: it often failed to capture real outcomes or create the right environment for teams
insights.locator: Hagen Tonnies: The shift also came from long-standing skepticism toward Scrum
